content.text: A quadrilateral has four sides, four angles, and four vertices. A parallelogram is a special type of quadrilateral in which both pairs of opposite sides are parallel.

content.title: Properties of a Diagonal
procedure: Cut out a parallelogram from a sheet of paper.
Cut it along a diagonal to obtain two triangles.
Place one triangle over the other and turn if necessary to compare them.
content.observation: The two triangles cover each other exactly, meaning they are congruent.
content.conclusion: A diagonal of a parallelogram divides it into two congruent triangles.

content.title: Theorem 8.1
content.text: A diagonal of a parallelogram divides it into two congruent triangles. Proof involves Alternate Interior Angles equal due to parallel opposite sides.

content.title: Theorem 8.2
content.text: In a parallelogram, opposite sides are equal.

content.title: Theorem 8.3 (Converse of 8.2)
content.text: If each pair of opposite sides of a quadrilateral is equal, then it is a parallelogram.

content.title: Theorem 8.4
content.text: In a parallelogram, opposite angles are equal.

content.title: Theorem 8.5 (Converse of 8.4)
content.text: If in a quadrilateral, each pair of opposite angles is equal, then it is a parallelogram.

content.title: Theorem 8.6
content.text: The diagonals of a parallelogram bisect each other.

content.title: Theorem 8.7 (Converse of 8.6)
content.text: If the diagonals of a quadrilateral bisect each other, then it is a parallelogram.
A rectangle is a parallelogram in which one angle is a right angle. By applying consecutive interior angle sum logic, it can be proven that each angle of a rectangle is a right angle.

content.title: Diagonals of a Rhombus
content.text: A rhombus has all four sides equal. By proving the congruence of the adjoining triangles formed by the intersecting diagonals, it can be shown that the diagonals of a rhombus are perpendicular to each other.

content.title: Angle Bisectors of a Parallelogram
content.text: The bisectors of the interior angles of a parallelogram form a rectangle at their intersections.

content.title: Mid-point Measurements
procedure: Draw a triangle ABC and mark the mid-points E and F of the two sides AB and AC respectively.
Join E and F. Measure EF and BC.
Measure angle AEF and angle ABC.
content.observation: EF is half the length of BC, and angle AEF equals angle ABC (meaning EF is parallel to BC).
content.conclusion: The line segment joining the mid-points of two sides of a triangle is parallel to the third side.

content.title: Theorem 8.8 (The Mid-point Theorem)
content.text: The line segment joining the mid-points of two sides of a triangle is parallel to the third side and is half of it.

content.title: Theorem 8.9
content.text: The line drawn through the mid-point of one side of a triangle parallel to another side bisects the third side.
Example Application: If D, E, and F are respectively the mid-points of sides AB, BC, and CA of a triangle ABC, then joining D, E, and F divides the original triangle into four congruent triangles.

glossary.definition: A closed two-dimensional polygon bounded by four straight sides and having four vertices and four angles.
A quadrilateral in which both pairs of opposite sides are parallel.
A parallelogram in which every angle is a right angle (90 degrees). Its diagonals are equal and bisect each other.
A parallelogram in which all four sides are equal in length. Its diagonals bisect each other at right angles.
A parallelogram which is both a rectangle (all angles are 90 degrees) and a rhombus (all sides are equal). Its diagonals bisect each other at right angles and are equal.
A quadrilateral in which exactly one pair of opposite sides is parallel.
A line segment connecting two non-consecutive vertices of a polygon.
To divide a line, an angle, or a shape into two exactly equal parts.
Triangles that are exactly the same in size and shape, meaning all corresponding angles and sides are equal.
A line that passes through two or more other lines in the same plane at distinct points.

summary_points: A diagonal of a parallelogram divides it into two congruent triangles.
In a parallelogram, opposite sides are equal, opposite angles are equal, and the diagonals bisect each other.
The converse statements of parallelogram properties are also true: if opposite sides are equal, opposite angles are equal, or diagonals bisect each other in a quadrilateral, it is a parallelogram.
The diagonals of a rectangle bisect each other and are equal in length.
The diagonals of a rhombus bisect each other at right angles.
The diagonals of a square bisect each other at right angles and are equal.
The Mid-point Theorem states that the line segment joining the mid-points of any two sides of a triangle is parallel to the third side and is half of its length.
The line drawn through the mid-point of one side of a triangle, parallel to another side, bisects the third side.
